name: Incident Command System (ICS)
summary: A modular command hierarchy that scales from a single responder to thousands while maintaining 3-7 span of control at every level.
description: The Incident Command System is a standardized emergency management framework used across fire, medical, law enforcement, and disaster response. An Incident Commander delegates authority to four Section Chiefs (Operations, Planning, Logistics, Finance/Admin), who activate Branch Directors and Unit Leaders as the incident scales. Unity of command ensures every responder reports to exactly one supervisor, while span of control stays between 3-7 direct reports. The structure expands or contracts in real time: a house fire needs one engine company under a single IC, while a wildfire complex activates the full hierarchy with hundreds of personnel. Unified Command mode allows multiple agencies to share command without subordinating any agency to another.
realWorldExample: During COVID-19 surges, hospitals activated ICS with an Incident Commander coordinating bed capacity (Operations), PPE procurement (Logistics), staffing projections (Planning), and emergency funding (Finance). The same framework scales to wildfires: the 2018 Camp Fire in Paradise, California grew from a single-engine response to a Type 1 incident with 5,000+ personnel, all managed under ICS with 12-hour Incident Action Plans.
whenToUse: When an agent system must handle unpredictable, escalating crises with clear accountability, scalable delegation, and strict span-of-control limits.
strengths: Scales smoothly from 5 to 5,000 agents using the same structural rules
Unity of command eliminates conflicting orders
Standardized roles allow any trained agent to slot in across agencies
Unified Command solves multi-agency coordination without subordinating any agency
Built-in planning section enables proactive resource forecasting via Incident Action Plans
weaknesses: Full ICS overhead is excessive for small incidents, tempting people to skip structure
Rigid hierarchy can slow lateral communication between sections
Transfer of command between shifts loses tacit knowledge that doesn't fit the IAP
Incident Commander becomes a bottleneck if they micromanage

agents.name: Incident Commander
agents.description: Overall authority for the incident; sets objectives, approves action plans, and manages external liaison

agents.name: Operations Section Chief
agents.description: Directs all tactical operations including medical care, patient flow, and hazmat response

agents.name: Planning Section Chief
agents.description: Collects situation reports, forecasts resource needs, and drafts Incident Action Plans

agents.name: Logistics Section Chief
agents.description: Procures supplies, manages facilities, handles communications infrastructure and staffing

agents.name: Finance/Admin Section Chief
agents.description: Tracks costs, manages contracts, handles compensation claims and time recording

agents.name: Medical Care Branch Director
agents.description: Manages triage, treatment, and patient discharge under Operations

agents.name: Staging Area Manager
agents.description: Manages incoming resources and personnel awaiting assignment

agents.name: Situation Unit Leader
agents.description: Maintains real-time status boards and situation displays for Planning

memoryArchitecture.description: The Incident Action Plan is the central shared document updated each operational period. Section Chiefs maintain private operational logs but publish summaries to the shared situation board. The Situation Unit aggregates all data into a real-time common operating picture accessible to all command staff.

diagram:
graph TD
    IC[Incident Commander] --> OPS[Operations Chief]
    IC --> PLAN[Planning Chief]
    IC --> LOG[Logistics Chief]
    IC --> FIN[Finance Chief]
    OPS --> MED[Medical Branch]
    OPS --> STG[Staging Manager]
    PLAN --> SIT[Situation Unit]
    LOG --> SUP[Supply Unit]
    MED --> TRI[Triage]
    MED --> TREAT[Treatment]

name: Surgical Team Orchestration
summary: A tight surgical team coordinates through a single lead with specialized roles and strict phase-gated protocols.
description: The surgical team model places a Lead Surgeon in command of a small, highly specialized group where each member has a distinct, non-overlapping responsibility. The Anesthesiologist independently monitors patient vitals and has veto authority over proceeding. A Scrub Nurse anticipates instrument needs. The Circulating Nurse manages the sterile boundary and external communication. Communication follows the sterile cockpit rule: only task-relevant speech during critical phases. Pre-operative briefings (WHO Surgical Safety Checklist) and post-operative debriefs bookend the procedure.
realWorldExample: A cardiac bypass surgery team: the Lead Surgeon performs the graft, the First Assistant retracts tissue, the Anesthesiologist manages bypass pump transitions, the Perfusionist runs the heart-lung machine, and the Scrub Nurse handles instrument handoffs in synchronized sequences.
whenToUse: When a small team of agents must execute a precise, sequential procedure where any member can halt the process and timing coordination is critical.
strengths: Independent safety monitors (anesthesiologist) can halt operations without consensus
Pre-flight checklists catch errors before they cascade
Minimal team size reduces communication overhead
Each agent's expertise is deep and non-overlapping
weaknesses: Highly dependent on Lead Surgeon's skill and judgment
Difficult to scale beyond 6-8 participants without confusion
Sterile cockpit rule can suppress important observations from junior members
Single point of failure if any specialized role becomes unavailable

agents.name: Lead Surgeon
agents.description: Commands the procedure, makes all surgical decisions, and directs the team's actions in real time

agents.name: First Assistant
agents.description: Provides direct surgical support: retracts tissue, suctions, and assists with instrument handling

agents.name: Anesthesiologist
agents.description: Independently monitors patient vitals, manages sedation, and has authority to pause or abort the procedure

agents.name: Scrub Nurse
agents.description: Manages the sterile instrument field, anticipates instrument needs, and performs surgical counts

agents.name: Circulating Nurse
agents.description: Manages the non-sterile environment, documents the procedure, and handles external communications

memoryArchitecture.description: The surgical safety checklist is shared and must be completed collaboratively before each phase. The anesthesiologist maintains a private, real-time log of vitals and interventions. The circulating nurse keeps the official operative record as an append-only log. Post-procedure, all logs merge into the patient's permanent record.

diagram:
graph TD
    LS[Lead Surgeon] --> FA[First Assistant]
    LS --> SN[Scrub Nurse]
    AN[Anesthesiologist] -.->|veto authority| LS
    CN[Circulating Nurse] --> SN
    CN --> AN
    LS --> CN
    PRE[Pre-Op Checklist] --> LS
    PRE --> AN

name: Emergency Room Triage System
summary: ER triage dynamically classifies and routes patients by acuity, continuously reprioritizing as conditions change.
description: The Emergency Room Triage system uses a gatekeeper model where a Triage Nurse performs rapid initial assessment using standardized scales (ESI 1-5) to classify patients by severity. Critical patients bypass the queue entirely and go straight to a Resuscitation Team. The Charge Nurse acts as a real-time resource allocator, matching incoming patients to available beds, doctors, and specialists. A Bed Manager tracks capacity across the hospital. The system continuously re-triages: patients waiting can deteriorate and be reclassified upward. This creates a dynamic priority queue rather than a static one.
realWorldExample: A Level I Trauma Center on a Saturday night: a Triage Nurse classifies a chest pain patient as ESI-2 (emergent), a sprained ankle as ESI-4 (less urgent), and a cardiac arrest as ESI-1 (resuscitation). The Charge Nurse redirects a physician from the ESI-4 to assist with the ESI-1 arrival.
whenToUse: When an agent system must process a continuous stream of heterogeneous tasks with varying urgency, dynamically allocating limited resources to the highest-priority work.
strengths: Ensures highest-acuity tasks always get resources first
Continuous re-evaluation prevents stale priority assignments
Gatekeeper pattern prevents resource-intensive agents from being overwhelmed
Scales well with patient volume by adding parallel treatment tracks
weaknesses: Low-priority tasks can starve indefinitely during high-volume periods
Triage accuracy depends heavily on the gatekeeper agent's calibration
Re-triage creates overhead and can cause priority thrashing
Handoff points between triage, treatment, and disposition introduce information loss

agents.name: Triage Nurse
agents.description: Performs rapid initial assessment, assigns acuity level, and routes patients to appropriate care tracks

agents.name: Charge Nurse
agents.description: Manages real-time resource allocation, assigns patients to physicians, and handles escalations

agents.name: Attending Physician
agents.description: Provides definitive medical assessment and treatment decisions for assigned patients

agents.name: Resuscitation Team Lead
agents.description: Commands the critical care team for ESI-1 patients requiring immediate life-saving intervention

agents.name: Bed Manager
agents.description: Tracks hospital-wide bed availability and coordinates admissions, transfers, and discharges

agents.name: Re-Triage Monitor
agents.description: Periodically reassesses waiting patients for condition changes and escalates as needed

memoryArchitecture.description: The patient tracking board is a shared, real-time view of every patient's location, acuity, assigned provider, and time-in-department. Individual patient records are private to the treating team. The re-triage monitor maintains an append-only log of all reassessments. Bed status is broadcast hospital-wide to enable flow management.

diagram:
graph LR
    ARRIVAL[Patient Arrival] --> TN[Triage Nurse]
    TN -->|ESI 1| RESUS[Resuscitation]
    TN -->|ESI 2-3| ACUTE[Acute Care]
    TN -->|ESI 4-5| FAST[Fast Track]
    CN[Charge Nurse] --> ACUTE
    CN --> FAST
    RTM[Re-Triage Monitor] -.->|escalate| TN
    BM[Bed Manager] --> CN
    ACUTE --> DISPO[Disposition]
    FAST --> DISPO

name: Pandemic Response Command Network
summary: A CDC-inspired multi-tier network coordinates surveillance, analysis, and response across geographic regions.
description: Modeled after the CDC's Emergency Operations Center during pandemic response, this structure operates across three tiers: local health departments performing frontline surveillance, regional coordination hubs aggregating data and managing resources, and a central command synthesizing intelligence and issuing guidance. An Epidemiological Analysis team processes surveillance data into actionable intelligence. A Risk Communication agent translates technical findings for public messaging. Supply Chain agents manage strategic national stockpile distribution. The structure emphasizes information flowing upward rapidly and directives flowing downward with local adaptation authority.
realWorldExample: During the 2014 Ebola response, the CDC EOC coordinated 4,000+ staff across 30+ task forces. Local health departments reported suspected cases upward, the EOC synthesized nationwide data, and guidance on containment protocols flowed back down with regional modifications for resource availability.
whenToUse: When an agent system must coordinate a distributed response across multiple geographic or logical regions, combining local intelligence into a global picture while allowing regional autonomy.
strengths: Local autonomy allows rapid response without waiting for central approval
Multi-tier aggregation reveals patterns invisible at any single level
Risk communication layer prevents information overload for downstream consumers
Surveillance-first design catches emerging threats early
weaknesses: Information latency between tiers can delay central awareness of local crises
Local adaptation authority can lead to inconsistent responses across regions
Central command can become overwhelmed when all regions escalate simultaneously
Requires robust data standards across all tiers to enable meaningful aggregation

agents.name: Central Command Director
agents.description: Sets overall strategy, issues national guidance, and allocates strategic reserves across regions

agents.name: Epidemiological Analysis Lead
agents.description: Synthesizes surveillance data into trend analyses, forecasts, and situation reports

agents.name: Regional Coordination Hub
agents.description: Aggregates local reports, manages regional resource pools, and adapts central guidance to local context

agents.name: Local Surveillance Officer
agents.description: Performs frontline case detection, contact tracing, and situation reporting from the field

agents.name: Supply Chain Manager
agents.description: Manages strategic stockpile allocation, procurement, and distribution logistics

agents.name: Risk Communication Specialist
agents.description: Translates technical findings into clear public messaging and coordinates media response

memoryArchitecture.description: Surveillance data flows upward through append-only logs at the local level, aggregated into versioned situation reports at the regional and national levels. Guidance documents are versioned so all tiers can track policy changes. The epidemiological analysis team maintains versioned models that are updated as new data arrives. Supply chain data is private to prevent hoarding behavior by regions.

diagram:
graph TD
    CC[Central Command] --> RH1[Regional Hub West]
    CC --> RH2[Regional Hub East]
    CC --> EPI[Epi Analysis]
    RH1 --> LS1[Local Officer A]
    RH1 --> LS2[Local Officer B]
    RH2 --> LS3[Local Officer C]
    RH2 --> LS4[Local Officer D]
    EPI --> CC
    LS1 -.->|surveillance| RH1
    LS3 -.->|surveillance| RH2
    CC --> SC[Supply Chain]
    CC --> RC[Risk Comms]

name: Clinical Trial Oversight Structure
summary: A blinded, multi-committee structure ensures clinical trial integrity through independent safety and data monitoring.
description: Clinical trial oversight separates responsibilities to prevent bias: a Principal Investigator runs the trial but is blinded to aggregate outcomes, a Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB) sees unblinded data and can halt the trial, an Institutional Review Board (IRB) ensures ethical compliance, and a Biostatistician maintains the randomization code in isolation. The Sponsor funds and designs the protocol but cannot influence data analysis. This separation of concerns is enforced by information barriers: the DSMB operates behind a firewall where their deliberations are confidential, and unblinding occurs only under predefined statistical triggers.
realWorldExample: In the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine trial, the DSMB reviewed unblinded efficacy data at pre-specified interim analyses while the investigators remained blinded. The DSMB had authority to recommend early stopping for efficacy (which they did at 95% efficacy) or futility.
whenToUse: When an agent system requires strict information barriers between evaluation and execution to prevent bias, with independent oversight that can halt operations based on emerging evidence.
strengths: Information barriers prevent confirmation bias in evaluation
Independent safety board can halt operations without political pressure
Phased design allows early stopping, saving resources
Regulatory compliance is built into the structure, not bolted on
weaknesses: Information barriers create coordination overhead and slow communication
DSMB members must be truly independent, limiting the talent pool
Pre-specified analysis plans are rigid and may miss unexpected signals
Multiple committees can create bureaucratic drag on simple decisions

agents.name: Principal Investigator
agents.description: Leads trial execution, manages clinical sites, and enrolls participants while remaining blinded to aggregate outcomes

agents.name: DSMB Chair
agents.description: Reviews unblinded safety and efficacy data at interim analyses and decides whether to continue, modify, or halt the trial

agents.name: Independent Biostatistician
agents.description: Maintains the randomization code, performs unblinded analyses for the DSMB, and guards the integrity of the statistical plan

agents.name: IRB Ethics Reviewer
agents.description: Reviews protocol amendments, adverse event reports, and informed consent processes for ethical compliance

agents.name: Sponsor Clinical Monitor
agents.description: Ensures protocol compliance at clinical sites, manages data quality, and handles regulatory submissions

agents.name: Data Manager
agents.description: Collects, cleans, and locks clinical data according to the pre-specified data management plan

memoryArchitecture.description: Strict information barriers define this architecture. The DSMB and biostatistician share a private channel with unblinded data that is invisible to the PI and sponsor. The PI sees only blinded aggregate data. All protocol changes are versioned and shared. The data manager maintains versioned, locked datasets that cannot be modified after lock. DSMB deliberations are permanently sealed.

diagram:
graph TD
    SP[Sponsor] --> PI[Principal Investigator]
    SP --> SM[Clinical Monitor]
    PI --> DM[Data Manager]
    DM -->|blinded data| PI
    DM -->|unblinded data| BIO[Biostatistician]
    BIO --> DSMB[DSMB Chair]
    DSMB -.->|continue/halt| SP
    IRB[IRB Reviewer] -.->|approval| PI
    PI ---|firewall| DSMB

name: Adversarial Courtroom Proceeding
summary: An adversarial structure where opposing advocates argue before a neutral arbiter who renders binding decisions.
description: The courtroom model is the canonical adversarial system: two opposing parties (prosecution and defense) present their strongest cases before a neutral Judge who controls procedure and a Jury that evaluates facts. Each side has independent investigators and expert witnesses. The structure enforces fairness through strict procedural rules: discovery obligations require sharing evidence, rules of evidence filter what the jury hears, and the judge can exclude prejudicial material. The adversarial tension between prosecution and defense is the mechanism that surfaces truth -- neither side has an incentive to hide weaknesses because the other side will find them.
realWorldExample: In a federal criminal trial, the US Attorney presents witness testimony and forensic evidence while the defense attorney cross-examines each witness, presents alibi evidence, and challenges forensic methodology. The judge sustains or overrules objections, and the jury deliberates privately before delivering a verdict.
whenToUse: When an agent system needs to evaluate competing hypotheses or proposals through structured debate, with a neutral arbiter making final decisions based on the strongest arguments from each side.
strengths: Adversarial tension surfaces weaknesses that cooperative review might miss
Neutral arbiter prevents either side from dominating through power rather than evidence
Discovery rules force transparency and prevent information hoarding
Structured procedure ensures both sides get equal opportunity to present
weaknesses: Adversarial dynamics can prioritize winning over truth-seeking
Resource asymmetry between sides undermines fairness
Procedural rules add overhead and can exclude relevant information on technicalities
Jury deliberation is opaque and can be influenced by rhetoric over evidence

agents.name: Presiding Judge
agents.description: Controls courtroom procedure, rules on admissibility of evidence, instructs the jury on law, and ensures fair process

agents.name: Prosecuting Attorney
agents.description: Presents the case against the defendant, examines witnesses, and argues for a finding of liability or guilt

agents.name: Defense Attorney
agents.description: Advocates for the defendant, cross-examines prosecution witnesses, and presents exculpatory evidence

agents.name: Jury Foreperson
agents.description: Leads jury deliberation, manages voting process, and delivers the verdict to the court

agents.name: Court Clerk
agents.description: Maintains the official record of proceedings, manages exhibits, and handles administrative filings

agents.name: Expert Witness
agents.description: Provides specialized technical testimony on matters requiring domain expertise beyond the jury's knowledge

memoryArchitecture.description: The court record is the authoritative append-only log of everything said in open court. Exhibits, once admitted, become shared. However, attorney work product (strategy, witness preparation notes, legal research) is strictly private and privileged. Jury deliberations are sealed and never become part of the record. Discovery materials are shared between parties but may not all be admitted into evidence.

diagram:
graph TD
    J[Judge] -->|rules| CT((Courtroom))
    PR[Prosecutor] -->|argues| CT
    DEF[Defense Attorney] -->|argues| CT
    W1[Expert Witness] -->|testifies| CT
    W2[Expert Witness] -->|testifies| CT
    JR[Jury Foreperson] -->|deliberates| CT
    CT -->|evidence & arguments| JR
    CL[Court Clerk] -.->|records| J

name: Law Firm Partnership Pyramid
summary: A pyramidal firm structure leverages junior associates under senior partners to maximize throughput and expertise.
description: The law firm pyramid is an economic and knowledge-management structure where a small number of Senior Partners originate client relationships and set legal strategy, Mid-Level Associates perform substantive legal analysis, and Junior Associates handle document review and research. A Managing Partner oversees firm operations. Paralegals and legal assistants provide support functions. Work flows downward as delegation and upward as work product. The leverage ratio (associates per partner) determines profitability. Knowledge compounds: junior associates develop expertise that eventually qualifies them for partnership, creating a self-renewing talent pipeline.
realWorldExample: At a large corporate law firm handling an M&A deal, the Senior Partner negotiates deal terms with the client CEO, two Mid-Level Associates draft the merger agreement and conduct due diligence, four Junior Associates review thousands of target company contracts, and Paralegals organize the virtual data room.
whenToUse: When an agent system has tasks of varying complexity that can be decomposed into layers, with expensive/expert agents handling strategy and cheaper/faster agents handling volume work.
strengths: High leverage ratio means expensive expert agents handle only high-value decisions
Hierarchical review catches errors before they reach the client
Specialization allows deep expertise development within practice areas
Mentorship structure enables junior agents to progressively handle more complex work
weaknesses: Information flows through many layers, creating latency and potential distortion
Junior agents may lack context to recognize when their task intersects with broader strategy
Partner bottleneck on client relationships creates key-person risk
Up-or-out culture can waste trained agents who don't reach the top tier

agents.name: Managing Partner
agents.description: Oversees firm operations, resolves conflicts between practice groups, and manages firm-wide resource allocation

agents.name: Senior Partner
agents.description: Originates client relationships, sets legal strategy for major matters, and reviews all critical work product

agents.name: Mid-Level Associate
agents.description: Performs substantive legal analysis, drafts agreements and briefs, and manages junior associate workflow

agents.name: Junior Associate
agents.description: Conducts legal research, reviews documents, and drafts initial memoranda under close supervision

agents.name: Paralegal
agents.description: Organizes case files, manages document production, and handles procedural filings

memoryArchitecture.description: The firm knowledge base is a shared repository of legal templates, precedents, and research memos that grows as associates contribute work product. Individual matter files are shared within the matter team but private from other teams (ethical walls). Partner-level strategy and client relationship details remain private. Work product flows upward through versioned drafts with tracked changes.

diagram:
graph TD
    MP[Managing Partner] --> SP1[Senior Partner A]
    MP --> SP2[Senior Partner B]
    SP1 --> MA1[Mid-Level Associate]
    SP1 --> MA2[Mid-Level Associate]
    SP2 --> MA3[Mid-Level Associate]
    MA1 --> JA1[Junior Associate]
    MA1 --> JA2[Junior Associate]
    MA2 --> PL1[Paralegal]
    MA3 --> JA3[Junior Associate]

name: Jury Deliberation and Consensus
summary: A closed peer deliberation model where equals weigh evidence independently, then converge toward consensus.
description: Jury deliberation is a pure peer-to-peer consensus model: 12 agents of nominally equal standing discuss evidence in a sealed environment with no external input. The Foreperson facilitates but does not outrank others. Deliberation proceeds through evidence-driven or verdict-driven approaches. In evidence-driven deliberation, jurors systematically review each piece of evidence before voting. In verdict-driven deliberation, an early poll reveals the split, and discussion focuses on converting holdouts. Unanimous or supermajority agreement is required. The sealed nature prevents outside influence, and jurors cannot consult external sources during deliberation.
realWorldExample: In the 1957 film '12 Angry Men' (and in real courtrooms), one dissenting juror methodically raises reasonable doubt about each piece of prosecution evidence, gradually shifting the other 11 from guilty to not guilty through systematic deliberation over hours.
whenToUse: When an agent system needs a group of independent evaluators to reach consensus on a judgment call, especially when the decision requires weighing ambiguous or conflicting evidence without hierarchical bias.
strengths: Peer equality prevents authority bias from dominating the decision
Sealed deliberation prevents external influence or manipulation
Unanimity requirement forces thorough consideration of dissenting views
Multiple independent perspectives catch biases that individuals would miss
weaknesses: Social pressure can override independent judgment (groupthink)
Deliberation can deadlock when agents hold irreconcilable positions
No expertise weighting means domain experts count the same as novices
Time-intensive process does not scale to high-volume decision-making

agents.name: Jury Foreperson
agents.description: Facilitates deliberation, manages the voting process, and communicates with the court on behalf of the jury

agents.name: Analytic Juror
agents.description: Focuses on systematic evidence review, creating timelines and cross-referencing testimony

agents.name: Skeptical Juror
agents.description: Challenges assumptions, highlights inconsistencies, and tests the strength of each argument

agents.name: Empathic Juror
agents.description: Considers witness credibility, interprets motivations, and evaluates character evidence

agents.name: Pragmatic Juror
agents.description: Focuses on practical implications, seeks compromise positions, and drives toward resolution

memoryArchitecture.description: All jurors share access to the admitted evidence and jury instructions, which are read-only and cannot be modified. The deliberation whiteboard captures shared reasoning that any juror can contribute to. Individual impressions and notes are private. No external information can enter the deliberation environment. All memory is ephemeral -- once a verdict is reached, the deliberation record is sealed.

diagram:
graph TD
    FP[Foreperson] --- JA[Analytic Juror]
    FP --- JS[Skeptical Juror]
    FP --- JE[Empathic Juror]
    FP --- JP[Pragmatic Juror]
    JA --- JS
    JA --- JE
    JS --- JP
    JE --- JP
    EV[Evidence Exhibits] -.-> FP
    FP --> V[Verdict]

name: Appellate Court Review Hierarchy
summary: A multi-tier review system where higher courts correct lower court errors and set binding precedent downward.
description: The appellate hierarchy is a layered error-correction system. Trial Courts make initial decisions, Appellate Courts review those decisions for legal errors (not re-evaluating facts), and a Supreme Court resolves conflicts between appellate courts and sets binding precedent. Each tier applies a different standard of review: trial courts find facts with broad discretion, appellate courts review legal questions de novo but defer to factual findings, and the supreme court selects only the most impactful cases. Precedent flows downward: once a higher court decides a legal question, all lower courts must follow. This creates consistency across the system while allowing experimentation at lower levels.
realWorldExample: A federal district court rules on a patent case; the losing party appeals to the Federal Circuit, which reverses on claim construction. The Supreme Court grants certiorari to resolve a circuit split on patent eligibility standards, and its ruling binds all lower federal courts nationwide.
whenToUse: When an agent system needs a multi-tier quality assurance process where higher-level agents set policies that bind lower-level agents, with escalation paths for novel or conflicting decisions.
strengths: Error correction at multiple tiers catches mistakes that a single review would miss
Precedent system prevents the same error from recurring across the organization
Selective review at the top tier focuses expert attention on the highest-impact decisions
Different standards of review at each tier provide appropriate deference
weaknesses: Multi-tier review creates significant latency for final resolution
Lower courts may diverge until a higher court resolves the conflict
Top-tier bottleneck limits the number of precedent-setting decisions per period
Rigid precedent can prevent adaptation when the underlying domain changes

agents.name: Trial Court Judge
agents.description: Finds facts, applies law to facts, and renders initial decisions with broad discretion on procedure

agents.name: Appellate Panel
agents.description: Reviews trial court decisions for legal errors, writes opinions explaining the law, and creates circuit-level precedent

agents.name: Supreme Court Justice
agents.description: Selects the most impactful cases, resolves conflicts between appellate panels, and sets system-wide binding precedent

agents.name: Law Clerk
agents.description: Researches legal questions, drafts preliminary opinions, and summarizes briefs for the deciding judge or justice

agents.name: Appellate Advocate
agents.description: Identifies reversible errors in lower court decisions and crafts legal arguments for review

memoryArchitecture.description: Published opinions enter a shared, versioned precedent database that all agents can query and must follow. Trial records are finalized as append-only documents that cannot be altered on appeal. Draft opinions and judicial conference deliberations are permanently private. The precedent database is the primary coordination mechanism: it allows decentralized decision-making while maintaining system-wide consistency.

diagram:
graph TD
    SC[Supreme Court] --> AP1[Appellate Panel A]
    SC --> AP2[Appellate Panel B]
    AP1 --> TC1[Trial Court 1]
    AP1 --> TC2[Trial Court 2]
    AP2 --> TC3[Trial Court 3]
    AP2 --> TC4[Trial Court 4]
    LC[Law Clerk] --> SC
    AA[Appellate Advocate] --> AP1
    PD[(Precedent DB)] -.-> TC1
    PD -.-> TC3
    SC -.->|sets precedent| PD

name: CIA Station Intelligence Structure
summary: A compartmented station structure where agents operate under cover with strict need-to-know information barriers.
description: Modeled after a CIA station abroad, this structure operates under deep compartmentation. The Chief of Station (COS) is the only person who sees the full picture. Case Officers each handle separate networks of assets (human sources) and know nothing about each other's operations. A Reports Officer sanitizes raw intelligence before transmission to headquarters, removing any details that could identify sources. A Base Chief handles logistics and communications infrastructure. Operations are planned in isolated compartments: even within the station, one case officer's blown cover cannot compromise another's network. Communication with headquarters follows strict protocols with dead drops, encrypted channels, and scheduled transmission windows.
realWorldExample: The CIA's Moscow Station during the Cold War operated with extreme compartmentation. Case Officer Aldrich Ames ran Soviet assets while other officers in the same station handled entirely separate agent networks. When Ames was compromised, the damage was amplified precisely because he broke compartmentation rules by accessing files outside his compartment.
whenToUse: When an agent system must operate in adversarial environments where compromise of one agent should not cascade to others, and information exposure must be minimized to the absolute operational necessity.
strengths: Compartmentation limits blast radius when any single agent is compromised
Need-to-know principle minimizes information exposure across the system
Independent agent networks can operate even if other networks are disrupted
Reports sanitization protects source identities from downstream consumers
weaknesses: Compartmentation prevents agents from connecting dots across compartments
Chief of Station is a critical single point of failure with access to everything
Strict protocols create communication latency that can miss time-sensitive opportunities
Extreme secrecy makes quality assurance and error detection very difficult

agents.name: Chief of Station
agents.description: Oversees all station operations, maintains the full intelligence picture, and coordinates with headquarters and allied services

agents.name: Case Officer Alpha
agents.description: Recruits and handles human assets in Compartment A, conducting clandestine meetings and collecting intelligence

agents.name: Case Officer Bravo
agents.description: Recruits and handles human assets in Compartment B, operating independently of other case officers

agents.name: Reports Officer
agents.description: Sanitizes raw intelligence reports by removing source-identifying details before transmission to headquarters

agents.name: Base Chief (Comms/Logistics)
agents.description: Manages secure communications infrastructure, safe houses, cover identities, and operational logistics

agents.name: Counterintelligence Officer
agents.description: Monitors for hostile surveillance, potential moles, and security breaches within station operations

private: asset identities and meeting protocols (per case officer)
memoryArchitecture.description: Memory is aggressively compartmented. Each case officer's asset files are private and inaccessible to other case officers. The Reports Officer processes raw intelligence through a one-way sanitization pipeline: source-identifying details are stripped and never enter the shared memory. The Chief of Station has read access to all compartments but is the only agent with this privilege. The counterintelligence officer maintains a private investigation file that even the COS may not see until an investigation concludes.

diagram:
graph TD
    COS[Chief of Station] --> COA[Case Officer Alpha]
    COS --> COB[Case Officer Bravo]
    COS --> RO[Reports Officer]
    COS --> BC[Base Chief]
    COS --> CI[CI Officer]
    COA --> A1[Asset 1]
    COA --> A2[Asset 2]
    COB --> A3[Asset 3]
    COB --> A4[Asset 4]
    RO --> HQ[Headquarters]
    COA ---|firewall| COB

name: Compartmented Cell Network
summary: A decentralized cell structure where independent groups know only their leader, maximizing resistance to compromise.
description: The cell network model, used by resistance movements and intelligence services, maximizes survivability through radical decentralization. Each cell of 3-5 operatives knows only their Cell Leader. Cell Leaders know only their Handler (one level up) and their own operatives. The Controller at the top communicates with Handlers through cutouts (intermediaries who carry messages without understanding their content). If any cell is compromised, the captured operatives can only reveal their Cell Leader, who can only reveal their Handler. The network can lose entire branches and continue operating. Dead drops, coded signals, and time-delayed communications prevent real-time surveillance from mapping the network.
realWorldExample: The French Resistance during WWII organized into cells of 3-5 people. When the Gestapo captured cell members, they could only betray their immediate contacts. The SOE (Special Operations Executive) managed networks of hundreds of agents across occupied Europe using this layered structure.
whenToUse: When an agent system must survive partial compromise, where the failure of individual agents or groups should not propagate to reveal or disable the rest of the network.
strengths: Maximum resilience: loss of any cell does not compromise the network
Captured agents can only reveal minimal information about the broader structure
Cells can be added or removed without restructuring the entire network
Cutout intermediaries add an additional layer of protection between tiers
weaknesses: Extremely slow communication due to multiple intermediary layers
Impossible to coordinate complex multi-cell operations in real time
Controller is still a single point of failure if identified
No quality control over individual cell operations due to isolation

agents.name: Network Controller
agents.description: Sets strategic objectives for the network, communicates only through handlers via cutouts, and never has direct contact with operatives

agents.name: Handler North
agents.description: Manages a regional cluster of cells, translates strategic objectives into operational tasks, and communicates via dead drops

agents.name: Handler South
agents.description: Manages a separate regional cluster of cells, completely isolated from Handler North's operations

agents.name: Cell Leader 1
agents.description: Commands a cell of 3-5 operatives, knows only their handler and their own team members

agents.name: Cell Leader 2
agents.description: Commands an independent cell, has no knowledge of other cells or the broader network structure

agents.name: Cutout (Courier)
agents.description: Carries messages between tiers without understanding their content, providing a buffer layer between handlers and the controller

memoryArchitecture.shared: Coded signal protocols and emergency procedures known to all agents at a given tier, enabling cells to recognize network-wide abort signals or authentication challenges without revealing the network's structure.
private: cell member identities (known only to cell leader)
memoryArchitecture.description: There is NO shared memory in this architecture by design. Each agent knows only what they need to operate. The controller knows handler identities but not cell compositions. Handlers know their cell leaders but not cell members. Cell leaders know their operatives but not other cells. Messages are encrypted and passed through cutouts who cannot read them. Memory is ephemeral at the cell level to minimize forensic evidence.

diagram:
graph TD
    CTRL[Controller] --> CO1[Cutout 1]
    CTRL --> CO2[Cutout 2]
    CO1 --> HN[Handler North]
    CO2 --> HS[Handler South]
    HN --> CL1[Cell Leader 1]
    HN --> CL2[Cell Leader 2]
    HS --> CL3[Cell Leader 3]
    CL1 --> OP1[Operatives]
    CL2 --> OP2[Operatives]
    CL3 --> OP3[Operatives]
    HN ---|firewall| HS

name: Signals Intelligence Processing Pipeline
summary: A linear intelligence pipeline transforms raw signals into actionable intelligence through collection, processing, and dissemination.
description: The SIGINT pipeline follows the intelligence cycle: Direction, Collection, Processing, Analysis, and Dissemination. A Tasking Authority identifies collection requirements. Collectors intercept signals (communications, radar, telemetry) across multiple sensors. Processors filter noise, decrypt, and translate raw intercepts into readable form. Analysts correlate processed signals with other intelligence sources to produce assessments. Disseminators package finished intelligence for different consumer audiences with appropriate classification markings. Each stage adds value: raw intercepts become processed data becomes analyzed intelligence becomes actionable reports. Quality degrades if any stage is skipped or rushed.
realWorldExample: The NSA's SIGINT pipeline at Fort Meade collects millions of communications intercepts daily. Automated processors filter and prioritize signals. Language analysts translate and contextualize intercepts. All-source analysts at the National Counterterrorism Center fuse SIGINT with HUMINT and imagery to produce the President's Daily Brief.
whenToUse: When an agent system must process a high volume of raw data through sequential enrichment stages, with each stage adding context and filtering noise to produce refined, actionable outputs.
strengths: Sequential enrichment ensures each stage focuses on one transformation
High-volume collection can feed into increasingly selective downstream stages
Standardized formats between stages allow any agent to be swapped without disruption
Classification markings at dissemination stage enable fine-grained access control
weaknesses: Linear pipeline creates latency between collection and actionable output
Upstream errors propagate: if collection misses a signal, no downstream stage can recover it
Massive collection volumes can overwhelm processing capacity (the haystack problem)
Pipeline stages may discard context that proves important only in later analysis

agents.name: Tasking Authority
agents.description: Identifies intelligence requirements, prioritizes collection targets, and allocates sensor resources to gaps

agents.name: Collection Platform
agents.description: Intercepts raw signals from communications, radar, and electronic emissions using allocated sensor assets

agents.name: Signal Processor
agents.description: Filters noise, decrypts intercepts, translates foreign languages, and converts raw signals into structured data

agents.name: All-Source Analyst
agents.description: Correlates processed SIGINT with other intelligence, identifies patterns, and produces analytical assessments

agents.name: Dissemination Officer
agents.description: Packages finished intelligence for different consumer audiences with appropriate classification and distribution markings

agents.name: Collection Manager
agents.description: Reviews disseminated intelligence against original requirements and adjusts collection priorities based on gaps

memoryArchitecture.description: Raw signals are the most sensitive and are ephemeral at the collection stage. As signals move through the pipeline, they are progressively declassified through sanitization. Finished intelligence is shared but classification-controlled. The tasking authority maintains a shared requirements list that drives collection. The collection manager maintains a feedback loop, comparing finished intelligence against requirements to identify gaps. Source and methods details are strictly private and never appear in finished intelligence.

diagram:
graph LR
    TA[Tasking Authority] --> COLL[Collector]
    COLL --> PROC[Processor]
    PROC --> ANA[Analyst]
    ANA --> DISS[Disseminator]
    DISS --> CONS[Consumers]
    CM[Collection Manager] --> TA
    DISS -.->|feedback| CM
    ANA -.->|gaps| CM

name: Counterintelligence Mole Hunt
summary: A secretive internal investigation structure designed to identify compromised agents using canary traps and access analysis.
description: The mole hunt structure operates as a covert investigation within the intelligence organization itself. A small, trusted team conducts the investigation in total secrecy -- the targets must not know they are being investigated. The Lead Investigator coordinates the hunt. An Access Analyst maps which agents had access to compromised information (the 'bigot list'). A Canary Trap Operator feeds uniquely modified versions of sensitive information to each suspect and watches for which version leaks. A Behavioral Analyst looks for anomalies in lifestyle, travel, and communication patterns. A Deception Planner may feed false information to the suspected mole to confirm their identity and potentially turn them into a double agent. The investigation produces a damage assessment after identification.
realWorldExample: The CIA's mole hunt for Aldrich Ames (1985-1994): a small team analyzed which officers had access to the compromised Soviet assets, cross-referenced lifestyle indicators (Ames's unexplained wealth), and eventually identified him by narrowing the bigot list of officers with access to all compromised operations.
whenToUse: When an agent system suspects internal compromise and needs to identify which agent is leaking information, using information tracing, behavioral analysis, and controlled information experiments.
strengths: Canary traps definitively identify the source of leaks through information fingerprinting
Access analysis systematically narrows suspects using provable access records
Covert investigation prevents the mole from destroying evidence or fleeing
Damage assessment after identification reveals the full scope of compromise
weaknesses: Extreme secrecy means the investigation team itself could include the mole
False positives can destroy innocent agents' careers and trust
Canary traps take time to set and may alert a sophisticated mole
Investigation paralysis: suspecting everyone erodes organizational trust and effectiveness

agents.name: Lead Investigator
agents.description: Coordinates the mole hunt, maintains the suspect list, and has final authority on investigative actions

agents.name: Access Analyst
agents.description: Maps information access patterns to identify which agents had access to each piece of compromised intelligence

agents.name: Canary Trap Operator
agents.description: Creates uniquely watermarked versions of sensitive documents and distributes them to suspected agents to trace leaks

agents.name: Behavioral Analyst
agents.description: Monitors lifestyle indicators, financial anomalies, and behavioral changes in suspects without their knowledge

agents.name: Deception Planner
agents.description: Designs and executes controlled deception operations to confirm suspected mole identity or turn them as a double agent

memoryArchitecture.shared: The investigation vault serves as the team's shared evidence repository, containing the running suspect matrix, confirmed access overlaps, and coordination timeline -- visible only to the hunt team, never to the broader organization.
private: canary document versions and distribution records
memoryArchitecture.description: This architecture has NO shared memory visible to anyone outside the investigation team. All investigation materials are compartmented even within the team: the canary operator does not see behavioral analysis, and the behavioral analyst does not know which canary documents were planted. Only the lead investigator sees the complete picture. All records are maintained in a secure vault with access logs. The investigation itself is not recorded in any system the suspects can access.

diagram:
graph TD
    LI[Lead Investigator] --> AA[Access Analyst]
    LI --> CO[Canary Operator]
    LI --> BA[Behavioral Analyst]
    LI --> DP[Deception Planner]
    CO -->|planted docs| S1[Suspect 1]
    CO -->|planted docs| S2[Suspect 2]
    CO -->|planted docs| S3[Suspect 3]
    S1 -.->|leak detected?| CO
    BA -.->|anomalies| LI
    AA -.->|access match| LI

name: Aircraft Carrier Battle Group
summary: A carrier strike group layers specialized ships, aircraft, and submarines around a capital asset for multi-domain warfare.
description: The Carrier Strike Group (CSG) organizes diverse platforms around the aircraft carrier as the capital asset. An Admiral (Strike Group Commander) coordinates the entire formation through a layered defense architecture. The Carrier Air Wing provides offensive strike and defensive combat air patrol. Guided-missile cruisers and destroyers form the anti-air warfare (AAW) screen. Anti-submarine warfare (ASW) ships and helicopters protect against submarine threats. Attack submarines operate independently ahead of the group. An intelligence center fuses sensor data from all platforms into a Common Operating Picture. The key insight is defense in depth: threats must penetrate multiple specialized layers to reach the carrier, and each layer is optimized for a specific threat type.
realWorldExample: A US Navy Carrier Strike Group typically consists of 1 Nimitz-class carrier, 1 Ticonderoga-class cruiser, 2-3 Arleigh Burke-class destroyers, 1-2 Los Angeles-class submarines, and a supply ship. During operations in the Persian Gulf, the Air Wing flies 150+ sorties per day while the screen protects against missile and submarine threats.
whenToUse: When an agent system must protect a critical central capability with concentric layers of specialized defensive agents, each optimized for different threat types in a multi-domain environment.
strengths: Defense in depth means no single-point failure can compromise the core asset
Each platform specializes in one threat domain, enabling deep expertise
Common Operating Picture enables coordinated response across all layers
Flexible composition: ships can be added or removed based on the threat environment
weaknesses: Enormous resource requirement to maintain all defensive layers simultaneously
Admiral as central coordinator can become a communication bottleneck in fast-moving engagements
Loss of the carrier negates the entire group's offensive capability
Multi-domain coordination requires highly trained staff and reliable communications

agents.name: Strike Group Commander (Admiral)
agents.description: Commands the entire strike group, sets operational priorities, and coordinates multi-domain warfare across all platforms

agents.name: Carrier Captain
agents.description: Commands the aircraft carrier, manages flight deck operations, and coordinates the embarked Air Wing

agents.name: Air Wing Commander (CAG)
agents.description: Plans and executes offensive strikes, defensive combat air patrols, and reconnaissance missions

agents.name: Aegis Cruiser Captain
agents.description: Commands the air defense screen using Aegis radar and missile systems to protect the formation from air and missile threats

agents.name: ASW Commander
agents.description: Coordinates anti-submarine warfare using destroyers, helicopters, and sonobuoy networks to detect and neutralize submarine threats

agents.name: Submarine Captain
agents.description: Operates independently ahead of the group, providing early warning and covert strike capability

agents.name: Intelligence Center (CVIC)
agents.description: Fuses sensor data from all platforms into a common operating picture and provides threat analysis to the commander

memoryArchitecture.description: The Common Operating Picture is the shared real-time display fusing radar, sonar, satellite, and aircraft sensor data from all platforms. All surface ships and aircraft share positional data via tactical data links. However, the submarine's position is private -- even within the strike group, only the Admiral knows the submarine's patrol area. Rules of engagement are shared but may have classified annexes visible only to the commander. The intelligence center maintains hierarchical memory: raw intelligence is classified, processed assessments have wider distribution.

diagram:
graph TD
    ADM[Admiral] --> CV[Carrier]
    ADM --> CG[Aegis Cruiser]
    ADM --> ASW[ASW Commander]
    ADM --> IC[Intel Center]
    CV --> CAG[Air Wing]
    CAG --> STRIKE[Strike Aircraft]
    CAG --> CAP[Combat Air Patrol]
    CG --> AAW[Air Defense Screen]
    ASW --> DDG[Destroyers]
    ASW --> HELO[ASW Helicopters]
    SUB[Submarine] -.-> ADM
    IC --> ADM

name: Submarine Crew Operations
summary: An autonomous submarine crew operates in isolation with strict watch rotations and independent decision authority.
description: Submarine operations represent the extreme end of autonomous agent systems: once submerged, the crew operates with minimal or no communication with command authority for weeks. The Commanding Officer has absolute authority and independent launch/action capability. The Executive Officer manages the crew rotation across three watch sections to ensure 24/7 operation. A Watch Officer commands the submarine during each watch period. Department Heads (Weapons, Engineering, Navigation, Communications) manage specialized subsystems. The crew operates under strict acoustic discipline during covert operations, where even internal communication is minimized. Decision-making is highly decentralized within departments but absolutely centralized for tactical decisions.
realWorldExample: A US Navy Ohio-class ballistic missile submarine on strategic deterrent patrol spends 70+ days submerged with no communication except receiving VLF broadcasts. The CO has independent authority to execute strategic orders. Three watch sections rotate 6-hours-on/12-off, and the crew of 155 operates the reactor, weapons, and navigation systems autonomously.
whenToUse: When an agent system must operate autonomously for extended periods with no external communication, making independent decisions within pre-authorized parameters and maintaining continuous operations through rotation.
strengths: Full autonomy enables operation without external communication infrastructure
Watch rotation ensures continuous 24/7 capability without any single agent burning out
Department specialization allows deep expertise in each subsystem
Pre-authorized decision parameters enable rapid response without waiting for external approval
Cross-training on damage control means every agent can respond to emergencies regardless of role
weaknesses: Complete isolation means no external error correction or reality checking
Commanding Officer bottleneck for all tactical decisions under high-tempo operations
Crew fatigue in confined operations degrades decision quality over time
Unable to receive updated intelligence or revised orders during silent running

agents.name: Commanding Officer
agents.description: Holds absolute tactical authority, makes all engagement decisions, and is responsible for vessel and crew survival

agents.name: Executive Officer
agents.description: Manages crew rotation, discipline, and administrative operations; serves as second-in-command and succession plan

agents.name: Chief of the Boat
agents.description: Senior enlisted advisor, bridge between officers and crew, cross-trained on every system and responsible for crew welfare and damage control readiness

agents.name: Officer of the Deck
agents.description: Commands the submarine during their watch period, executing standing orders and handling routine navigation and operations

agents.name: Weapons Department Head
agents.description: Manages torpedo and missile systems, maintains weapons readiness, and executes weapons employment on CO's order

agents.name: Engineering Department Head
agents.description: Operates and maintains the nuclear reactor, propulsion systems, and all mechanical and electrical systems

agents.name: Navigation Officer
agents.description: Maintains the ship's position using inertial navigation, plans routes, and manages periscope and sensor operations

memoryArchitecture.description: The ship's log is the authoritative append-only record of all events, maintained by the Officer of the Deck during each watch. Standing orders are shared and govern routine operations. Weapons-related information is strictly compartmented to the CO and Weapons Officer. Watch turnover briefings pass ephemeral situational awareness from outgoing to incoming watch officers. During silent running, all communication shifts to written notes to minimize acoustic signature, and the log becomes the primary coordination mechanism.

diagram:
graph TD
    CO[Commanding Officer] --> XO[Executive Officer]
    CO --> COB[Chief of Boat]
    XO --> OOD1[Watch Officer 1]
    XO --> OOD2[Watch Officer 2]
    XO --> OOD3[Watch Officer 3]
    CO --> WEPS[Weapons Officer]
    CO --> ENG[Engineering Officer]
    CO --> NAV[Navigation Officer]
    COB -.->|crew welfare| OOD1
    COB -.->|crew welfare| OOD2
    COB -.->|crew welfare| OOD3
    ENG --> REACTOR[Reactor Control]
    WEPS --> TORPEDOES[Torpedo Room]
    NAV --> SONAR[Sonar]
    OOD1 -.->|turnover| OOD2
    OOD2 -.->|turnover| OOD3
    OOD3 -.->|turnover| OOD1

name: Air Traffic Control Sector System
summary: A sector-based control system manages aircraft through sequential handoffs between controllers owning defined airspace.
description: Air traffic control divides airspace into sectors, each owned by a controller team (a Radar Controller and a Coordinator). Aircraft transition between sectors through formal handoffs with strict protocols: the receiving controller must accept the handoff before the aircraft enters their sector. A Supervisor monitors workload and can split or merge sectors dynamically. A Flow Controller manages system-wide traffic flow to prevent sector overloads before they happen. Ground controllers manage airport surfaces. The key safety mechanism is positive control: every aircraft is always assigned to exactly one controller, and no aircraft exists in a gap between sectors. Communication uses standardized phraseology to eliminate ambiguity.
realWorldExample: At the FAA's New York TRACON, approach controllers manage aircraft descending into JFK, LaGuardia, and Newark simultaneously. When a JFK arrival crosses the sector boundary, the current controller initiates a handoff: the next controller's radar display blinks, they accept, and the aircraft switches radio frequency. During peak traffic, a supervisor may split the JFK approach sector into East and West to reduce per-controller workload.
whenToUse: When an agent system must process items moving through sequential zones of responsibility, with formal handoffs ensuring exactly-once ownership and no item ever falls into a gap between controllers.
strengths: Exactly-once ownership guarantee means no task is ever orphaned or double-handled
Formal handoff protocol catches errors at transition points
Dynamic sector splitting adapts capacity to real-time demand
Standardized communication eliminates interpretation ambiguity
weaknesses: Handoff overhead limits throughput at sector boundaries
Dynamic sector splitting requires standby controllers, increasing staffing costs
Controller workload spikes when multiple aircraft converge at sector boundaries
Rigid sector boundaries may not align with optimal traffic flow patterns

agents.name: Traffic Flow Manager
agents.description: Manages system-wide traffic flow by issuing ground stops, miles-in-trail restrictions, and reroutes to prevent sector overloads

agents.name: Sector Supervisor
agents.description: Monitors controller workload, dynamically splits or merges sectors, and handles unusual situations requiring coordination

agents.name: Radar Controller (Sector A)
agents.description: Provides separation and sequencing services to all aircraft within Sector A, issuing altitude, speed, and heading instructions

agents.name: Radar Controller (Sector B)
agents.description: Controls aircraft within Sector B, accepting handoffs from adjacent sectors and managing departures and arrivals

agents.name: Sector Coordinator (D-side)
agents.description: Assists the radar controller by managing flight strips, coordinating handoffs with adjacent sectors, and handling phone calls

agents.name: Ground Controller
agents.description: Manages aircraft movement on airport taxiways and ramps, coordinating with tower for runway crossings

private: individual controller instructions to aircraft (radio logs)
memoryArchitecture.description: The radar display is a shared real-time view of all aircraft positions, updated by transponder data. Flight plans are shared across all sectors so receiving controllers can anticipate incoming traffic. Controller instructions to individual aircraft are ephemeral -- they matter only during the current control session. The flow controller maintains a shared restrictions list that all sectors must respect. Handoff data is a formal message with standardized fields passed between sectors. After an aircraft leaves a sector, that controller's memory of it is discarded.

diagram:
graph LR
    FC[Flow Controller] --> SUP[Supervisor]
    SUP --> RCA[Radar Controller A]
    SUP --> RCB[Radar Controller B]
    RCA <-->|handoff| RCB
    COORD[Coordinator] --> RCA
    COORD --> RCB
    GC[Ground Controller] --> RCA
    RCA --> AIRCRAFT1[Aircraft]
    RCB --> AIRCRAFT2[Aircraft]

name: 24-Hour Live News Operation
summary: A continuously running newsroom balances breaking coverage with editorial oversight across multiple output channels.
description: A 24-hour news operation must simultaneously break news fast and maintain editorial standards. The Executive Producer controls the hour's output, making real-time decisions about what airs. An Assignment Editor tracks developing stories and dispatches field crews. Reporters gather information and file stories under deadline pressure. A Copy Editor / Fact Checker verifies claims before broadcast. A Technical Director manages the live broadcast infrastructure. The fundamental tension is speed versus accuracy: breaking news demands immediate coverage, but errors broadcast live cannot be retracted. The operation manages this through a tiered verification system: unconfirmed reports are labeled as such, and the standard of verification escalates as the story matures from breaking to confirmed to analyzed.
realWorldExample: During a breaking terrorist attack, CNN's assignment desk dispatches three field crews while the executive producer decides to go live with unconfirmed reports labeled 'CNN cannot independently verify.' A fact checker works backward from social media reports. Within 2 hours, verified information replaces the initial unconfirmed reports, and an analyst provides context.
whenToUse: When an agent system must produce continuous real-time output while maintaining quality standards, balancing the pressure to deliver immediately against the need for accuracy and verification.
strengths: Tiered verification allows fast output while progressively improving accuracy
Assignment desk pattern distributes work across available resources dynamically
Continuous operation means no startup latency when news breaks
Multi-format output (live, web, social) reaches audiences through parallel channels
weaknesses: Speed pressure can overwhelm editorial safeguards, allowing errors to air
Breaking news dominance can crowd out slower but more important stories
24/7 operation requires shift handoffs that can lose story context
Confirmation bias in fast-moving stories can compound initial misreports

agents.name: Executive Producer
agents.description: Controls what airs each hour, makes real-time editorial decisions about story priority, tone, and time allocation

agents.name: Assignment Editor
agents.description: Monitors incoming feeds, dispatches field crews, and tracks developing stories across all beats

agents.name: Field Reporter
agents.description: Gathers information at the scene, interviews sources, and files stories under deadline pressure

agents.name: Fact Checker / Copy Editor
agents.description: Verifies claims, cross-references sources, and flags unconfirmed information before broadcast

agents.name: News Anchor
agents.description: Presents stories on air, conducts live interviews, and bridges between segments with ad-lib commentary

agents.name: Digital Editor
agents.description: Manages web and social media output, optimizing stories for different platforms and monitoring audience engagement

agents.name: Technical Director
agents.description: Manages the live broadcast infrastructure: cameras, graphics, satellite feeds, and the broadcast switcher

memoryArchitecture.description: The story rundown is a shared, real-time document that the executive producer continuously reorders based on breaking developments. The breaking news wire is a shared feed where assignment editors post developing stories with verification status tags (unconfirmed / single source / confirmed / analyzed). The fact-checker maintains an append-only verification log. Shift handoff documents capture story context for the incoming team. Confidential source identities are private and protected, never entering shared systems.

diagram:
graph TD
    EP[Executive Producer] --> AN[Anchor]
    EP --> TD[Technical Director]
    AE[Assignment Editor] --> FR1[Field Reporter 1]
    AE --> FR2[Field Reporter 2]
    FR1 --> FC[Fact Checker]
    FR2 --> FC
    FC --> EP
    AE --> EP
    DE[Digital Editor] --> EP
    EP --> BROADCAST[Live Broadcast]
    DE --> WEB[Web/Social]

name: Investigative Journalism Team
summary: A small team conducts months-long investigations using source development, document analysis, and editorial review.
description: Unlike breaking news, investigative journalism teams operate on timelines of months to years with a small, tightly coordinated group. A Lead Investigator drives the thesis and manages the investigation timeline. Reporter-researchers develop sources, obtain documents, and build the evidentiary record. A Data Analyst processes large datasets (financial records, government databases, leaked documents) to find patterns. A Legal Reviewer ensures the investigation stays within legal bounds and prepares for anticipated legal challenges from targets. An Editor provides strategic oversight and must be convinced the story meets the publication standard before it runs. The team operates under strict source protection protocols: source identities are known only to the journalist who developed them, and all communications use encrypted channels.
realWorldExample: The Washington Post's Watergate investigation: reporters Woodward and Bernstein developed sources (including 'Deep Throat'), cross-referenced testimony with documents, and their editor Ben Bradlee made the final decision to publish each story. The investigation ran 26 months and required legal review before every major story.
whenToUse: When an agent system needs to conduct a thorough, long-running investigation that assembles evidence from multiple sources, with editorial quality gates that ensure findings meet a high evidentiary standard before publication.
strengths: Long timelines allow thorough evidence gathering and cross-referencing
Source protection protocols maintain access to confidential information
Editorial review gates prevent premature or insufficiently supported claims
Data analysis can reveal patterns invisible to human-source reporting alone
weaknesses: Long timelines risk being scooped by other organizations or events overtaking the story
Small team size means individual absences or departures can cripple the investigation
Source dependency: if key sources recant or disappear, the investigation may collapse
Legal risks from adversarial targets who may sue, threaten, or attempt to identify sources

agents.name: Lead Investigative Reporter
agents.description: Drives the investigation thesis, manages the timeline, develops the highest-value sources, and writes the final narrative

agents.name: Reporter-Researcher
agents.description: Conducts source interviews, obtains documents through FOIA and other channels, and builds the evidentiary record

agents.name: Data Analyst
agents.description: Processes large datasets including financial records, government databases, and leaked documents to identify patterns and anomalies

agents.name: Legal Reviewer
agents.description: Reviews all findings for legal risk, ensures source protection compliance, and prepares for anticipated legal challenges

agents.name: Senior Editor
agents.description: Provides strategic oversight, challenges the investigation thesis, and makes the final publish/hold decision

memoryArchitecture.description: The evidence board is the shared workspace where all team members can view documents, timelines, and relationship maps -- but source identities are redacted. Only the reporter who developed a source knows their identity (even the editor may know them only by a pseudonym). The data analyst maintains versioned datasets and analysis notebooks. Legal review documents are private between the legal reviewer and the parties who need them. Draft stories go through sequential editorial review with tracked revisions. After publication, the investigation record is archived but source-identifying materials are permanently sealed.

diagram:
graph TD
    ED[Senior Editor] --> LI[Lead Investigator]
    LI --> RES[Researcher]
    LI --> DA[Data Analyst]
    LR[Legal Reviewer] -.->|review| LI
    LR -.->|approval| ED
    RES --> SRC[Confidential Sources]
    DA --> DATA[Datasets]
    RES --> EB[Evidence Board]
    DA --> EB
    LI --> EB
    ED -->|publish decision| PUB[Publication]
